cursor.name: Cursor
cursor.tagline: AI-first code editor built for pair-programming with AI
features: Built-in AI chat with codebase context
Imports from VS Code extensions and settings
pros: Composer agent is incredibly powerful for multi-file changes
Fast and responsive even on large projects
cons: Requires separate subscription on top of IDE
Occasionally slow AI responses under heavy load
Some workflows feel forced into AI-first paradigm
cursor.bestFor: Developers who want the most powerful AI coding assistant and are willing to pay for it

windsurf.name: Devin Desktop
windsurf.tagline: Formerly Windsurf — AI-powered IDE with agent command center by Cognition
features: Agent Command Center for managing fleets of local + cloud agents
Devin Cloud for cloud-based AI agents
SWE 1.6 model — latest AI coding model
Supports OpenAI, Claude, Gemini frontier models
VS Code fork with familiar UX (Windsurf heritage)
pros: Powerful agentic platform with local + cloud agents
SWE 1.6 is one of the best coding models available
Access to multiple frontier models (Claude, GPT, Gemini)
Free tier with unlimited inline edits and tab completions
cons: Rebranded from Windsurf — some UX disruption during transition
Pro plan same price as Cursor ($20/mo) — no longer the budget pick
Team pricing jumped significantly ($80/mo base)
Cloud agent usage can consume quota quickly
Newer platform — still finding its footing post-rebrand
windsurf.bestFor: Developers who want an all-in-one agentic platform with both local and cloud AI agents, backed by Cognition's Devin AI

copilot.name: GitHub Copilot
copilot.tagline: Your AI pair programmer from GitHub and OpenAI

pricing.freePrice: Free for verified students & open source

features: Copilot Chat with GPT-4o and Claude
pros: Largest user base and most mature product
Works inside any IDE (VS Code, JetBrains, Neovim, etc.)
Excellent inline completions — fast and accurate
Free for students and popular open-source maintainers
cons: Chat is good but not as powerful as Cursor's Composer
Multi-file editing is newer and less polished
Microsoft/OpenAI lock-in concerns
Privacy: code sent to servers by default
copilot.bestFor: Teams already using GitHub who want the most widely-supported AI coding assistant across all IDEs

codeium.name: Codeium
codeium.tagline: Free AI code completion — now part of Cognition's Devin ecosystem
features: Codeium command for natural language search
Now integrated into Devin Desktop IDE
pros: Free tier still available with unlimited completions
Fast completions without waiting on frontier models
cons: Standalone product overshadowed by Devin Desktop rebrand
Chat capabilities less sophisticated than Cursor/Copilot
Pro pricing jumped to $20/mo (was $12)
Multi-file agent editing less polished than Cursor Composer
codeium.bestFor: Developers who want free AI completions in any IDE, or enterprises needing on-premise deployment for privacy

cline.name: Cline
cline.tagline: Autonomous AI coding agent that lives inside your IDE
features: Fully autonomous coding agent in VS Code
Bring your own LLM (Claude, GPT-4, local models)
Can run terminal commands, edit files, browse web
No subscription — pay only for API usage
pros: No vendor lock-in on AI model
Can use local models for zero cost
Extremely flexible — adapts to your workflow
cons: Requires API key setup (not plug-and-play)
Can make mistakes without guardrails
API costs can add up for heavy use
Less polished UX than commercial alternatives
Requires more supervision during complex tasks
cline.bestFor: Power users who want maximum control over their AI agent and don't mind managing API keys

tabnine.name: Tabnine
tabnine.tagline: AI code assistant that runs locally for maximum privacy
features: Local AI model — code never leaves your machine
Team learning from your private code
pros: Best privacy option — everything runs locally
No internet connection needed for completions
Team learning adapts to your codebase
Good completion quality for common patterns
cons: Local model less capable than cloud-based GPT-4/Claude
Chat and agent features less powerful
Slower to adopt new AI capabilities
Completions can be repetitive on larger codebases
tabnine.bestFor: Teams with strict security/privacy requirements who need code assistance without sending code to cloud services

continue.name: Continue
continue.tagline: Open-source AI code assistant that runs inside your IDE — fast, private, extensible.
features: Open-source AI code assistant for VS Code and JetBrains
Uses local or cloud LLMs (Claude, GPT, local via Ollama)
Inline completions and chat with codebase context
Tab autocomplete with multiple model support
Lightweight and fast — no heavy IDE needed
pros: Runs locally with Ollama for zero cost
cons: Less polished than Cursor or Copilot
Requires LLM configuration
Smaller feature set than commercial tools
Documentation could be better
continue.bestFor: Developers who want a free, open-source AI coding assistant with full model control

cody.name: Sourcegraph Cody
cody.tagline: AI coding assistant with deep codebase understanding powered by Sourcegraph's code intelligence.
features: Bring your own LLM or use Cody's models
Code graph intelligence for cross-repo understanding
pros: Best codebase understanding of any AI assistant
cons: Setup can be complex for private repos
Sometimes slow on very large codebases
Less known than Cursor or Copilot
Chat can be verbose
cody.bestFor: Teams with large, complex codebases who need deep cross-repo AI understanding

codewhisperer.name: Amazon Q Developer
codewhisperer.tagline: Amazon's AI coding companion — free for individuals with CodeWhisperer heritage.
features: Free AI code suggestions for individuals
pros: AWS integration if you're in that ecosystem
cons: Not as polished as Cursor or Copilot for general coding
AWS-centric — less useful outside AWS
Code suggestions less accurate than competitors
Chat features are basic
codewhisperer.bestFor: AWS developers or teams needing free AI coding with built-in security scanning

vercel.name: Vercel
vercel.tagline: Deploy web apps in seconds. The native platform for Next.js, React, and more.
pros: Automatic scaling to zero when idle
cons: Cold starts on serverless functions
Vendor lock-in for some features
Pricing can escalate with traffic
Limited serverless function execution time
vercel.bestFor: Frontend developers deploying Next.js/React apps who want zero-config deployments

railway.name: Railway
railway.tagline: Deploy anything. Backend, databases, cron jobs — all in one platform.
pros: Databases included — no separate provider needed
Great for MVPs and side projects
cons: Pricing meter-based — can surprise at scale
Limited regions compared to big cloud providers
No custom domains on free tier
Less mature CI/CD than Vercel/Netlify
railway.bestFor: Developers who want to deploy full-stack apps (backend + DB) without managing infrastructure

hostinger.name: Hostinger
hostinger.tagline: Affordable web hosting with VPS, shared hosting, and domain registration.
pros: Good for beginners and small projects
cons: Shared hosting limited for production apps
VPS requires Linux knowledge
Not ideal for modern serverless/Jamstack
Upselling on renewal prices
hostinger.bestFor: Beginners and small businesses who need affordable shared hosting with domain included

netlify.name: Netlify
netlify.tagline: Build, deploy, and host your static sites and web apps with Git-based workflow.
pros: Excellent for static sites and JAMstack
Built-in form handling saves backend work
cons: Less suited for dynamic server-rendered apps than Vercel
Serverless functions limited to 10s on free tier
No built-in databases
Build minutes limited on free tier
netlify.bestFor: Frontend developers building static sites, blogs, or documentation who need built-in forms and auth

render.name: Render
render.tagline: Modern cloud platform for building and running web apps and APIs without configuration.
features: Web services, background workers, cron jobs
pros: Great value at $7/mo for starter plan
Similar to Railway but more established
cons: Free tier spins down after 15 min inactivity
Cold starts on free plan
Less generous free tier than Vercel
Limited regions
render.bestFor: Developers who need full-stack deployment with managed databases at a reasonable price

flyio.name: Fly.io
flyio.tagline: Run your apps close to your users with global edge deployment in 30+ regions.
pros: Very fast cold starts with Firecracker VMs
Good for APIs and background workers
cons: Steeper learning curve than Vercel/Railway
Less polished dashboard
Debugging can be tricky
Support documentation needs improvement
flyio.bestFor: Developers building APIs or real-time apps that need global low-latency deployment

digitalocean.name: DigitalOcean
digitalocean.tagline: Simple cloud hosting with Droplets, managed databases, App Platform, and GPU compute.
pros: Simplest cloud provider to get started with
cons: You manage servers yourself (Droplets)
Less automated than Vercel/Railway
Support can be slow for complex issues
Fewer regions than AWS/GCP
digitalocean.bestFor: Developers who want full server control at a predictable price, or teams needing GPU compute for AI/ML
